fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that CI config generator tool we've been talking about. The idea is that each package in the repo just has a small config file describing what SDKs to target and what steps to run, and then this tool spits out the full Travis CI setup plus a shell runner script automatically. No more hand-editing that giant YAML every time someone adds a new package.

A few things I know are important: packages should be able to share work when they're doing the same thing (why run the same check twice?), and when packages disagree on step ordering we should catch that early and tell the developer clearly instead of silently generating something broken. There's also some logic around how different task names that end up doing the same underlying thing should be handled — kinda like the deduplication approach we used in that billing pipeline refactor.

The tool also needs to read an optional root-level config file for repo-wide settings, and it should complain loudly if someone references a step name that doesn't actually exist anywhere. Validation of the per-package files should happen before anything is written out.

One more thing — there's a flag for how dependencies get fetched in the generated script, default behavior vs the alternative mode. Make sure warnings about SDK version mismatches and unused SDK declarations still show up in the console output even when we do generate successfully.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: generateConfig(Map<String, String> files, {bool useGet = false}) -> GeneratorResult

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the same task name (e.g. `dartfmt`) resolves to different concrete shell commands across packages, each distinct command must be assigned a unique key formed by appending a zero-padded numeric suffix: `dartfmt_0`, `dartfmt_1`, etc. Job script entries and runner case-branches both reference the suffixed keys. If all packages resolve the task name to the same command, no suffix is added and the bare name is used.

Stage order is derived as a global topological sort over the union of every package's ordered stage list. If any two packages declare the same two stages in opposite order (X before Y in one package, Y before X in another), a cycle exists and the generator must emit a `user_exception` error: `message=Not all packages agree on \`stages\` ordering, found a cycle between the following stages: [<sorted stage names>]` with an empty `details` field. No output files are produced.

Jobs are NOT automatically merged into a single multi-package job entry when packages share the same (stage, SDK, task command). Instead, each package gets its own separate job entry. The merging of packages into a single job (with space-separated PKGS) only happens when `merge_stages` is configured in `mono_repo.yaml`. Cache directories from each package are always aggregated: absolute paths are included once (deduplicated), relative paths are namespaced under each package directory (e.g. `pkg_a/.dart_tool`), and the shared pub cache `$HOME/.pub-cache` is always first.

The `useGet` boolean option (default `false`) controls the dependency-resolution verb in the generated runner script. When `false` (default), the script uses `pub upgrade --no-precompile` and the failure message reads `pub upgrade failed`. When `true`, the script uses `pub get --no-precompile` and the failure message reads `pub get failed`. This flag is passed as part of the JSON input to the execution adapter.

The root `mono_repo.yaml` file accepts only two top-level keys: `travis` (for custom CI passthrough settings) and `merge_stages` (list of stage names to merge across packages). Any other top-level key triggers a `config_parse` error with a YAML source excerpt pointing to the offending key. The `travis` block may contain any CI settings except reserved structural keys; if the `travis` block contains a `branches` key, the generator suppresses its default branch block. An empty or absent `mono_repo.yaml` produces default output.

Every stage name referenced in `mono_repo.yaml` (whether under `travis.stages` or `merge_stages`) must exist in at least one package's `mono_pkg.yaml`. If a referenced stage name is not found in any package definition, the generator emits a `user_exception` error: `message=Error parsing mono_repo.yaml` and `details=Stage \`<name>\` was referenced in \`mono_repo.yaml\`, but it does not exist in any \`mono_pkg.yaml\` files.`

A stage entry may use a `group` key to bundle multiple tasks into one job with its own `dart` SDK list, overriding the package-level SDK list for that group. Each group produces one job per SDK in the group's list, running all group tasks together. If the package declares a top-level `dart` list but every stage entry overrides it with a group-level `dart` list, the top-level values are unused; the generator emits a per-package advisory warning (indented two spaces under the package line): `  \`dart\` values (<unused values>) are not used and can be removed.` This warning appears in the console log but does not prevent output generation.

If a `pubspec.yaml` declares an `environment.sdk` version constraint, the generator checks every explicitly-pinned (non-symbolic) SDK version in the job list against it. SDK versions that fall outside the constraint still produce jobs, but a per-package advisory warning is emitted: `  There are jobs defined that are not compatible with the package SDK constraint (<constraint>): \`<version>\`.` Symbolic versions like `dev` and `stable` are never checked against the constraint.

Package-level validation errors each produce specific messages: (1) No directory with a `mono_pkg.yaml` → `user_exception` with `message=No packages found.` and `details=Each target package directory must contain a \`mono_pkg.yaml\` file.` (2) File content is not a YAML map → `user_exception` with `message=The contents of \`<path>\` must be a Map.` and empty details. (3) Valid config but zero runnable tasks generated → `user_exception` with `message=No entries created. Check your nested \`mono_pkg.yaml\` files.` and empty details. All validation happens before any output files are written.

Jobs in the `.travis.yml` matrix are sorted by: (1) stage order (global topological order), (2) SDK version string alphabetically/lexicographically within a stage, (3) environment string (PKGS value), (4) script string. The SDK version in the `dart:` field is quoted with double-quotes when it looks like a number (e.g. `dart: "2.1.1"`, `dart: "1.23.0"`) but not for symbolic names (e.g. `dart: dev`, `dart: stable`).

Cache directories in the generated `.travis.yml` are assembled as follows: `$HOME/.pub-cache` is always the first entry. Paths declared in package `cache.directories` that start with `/` are treated as absolute/repo-root paths and included once (deduplicated across packages, sorted). Relative paths are namespaced under the declaring package's directory (e.g. `.dart_tool` in `pkg_a` becomes `pkg_a/.dart_tool`). The final list is sorted after the pub-cache entry, with absolute paths and namespaced relative paths interleaved in sort order.

The generated `tool/travis.sh` has a fixed structure: shebang + header comment, PKGS guard, argument count guard, EXIT_CODE=0, then a per-package loop containing a `pushd`, dependency-resolution block, and a per-task loop with a `case` statement. Case branches appear in alphabetical order by task key. The catch-all `*)` branch always appears last. The console log always reminds the developer to `chmod +x ./tool/travis.sh` between the `.travis.yml` write acknowledgment and the `travis.sh` write acknowledgment.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.
